text.title: BABOK Knowledge Areas — Workflow and Competencies
text.summary: KA relationships, typical BA workflow sequence for greenfield and change-request contexts, and underlying competencies across all KAs.

section.title: Knowledge Area Relationships
section.p: The six KAs are not sequential — they interact throughout the project lifecycle:

list.item: KA-1 (Planning) defines the approach that governs how all other KAs operate.
KA-2 (Elicitation) and KA-5 (Analysis) feed each other iteratively: elicitation produces raw material, analysis structures and validates it, gaps trigger more elicitation.
KA-3 (Lifecycle) operates continuously — tracing, maintaining, and approving requirements as they evolve through all other KAs.
KA-4 (Strategy) provides the current and future state context that KA-2 and KA-5 must operate within.
KA-6 (Evaluation) assesses the built solution and feeds learnings back into KA-1 and KA-3 for the next cycle.

rule.statement: For a greenfield engagement, sequence KAs as: KA-1 → KA-4 → KA-2 → KA-5 (model) → KA-3 (prioritize and approve) → KA-5 (design) → build → KA-6.
rule.rationale: Starting without KA-1 produces unplanned elicitation; starting without KA-4 produces requirements disconnected from business strategy. KA-3 between modeling and design ensures only approved requirements enter the design phase.

list.item: KA-1: Plan BA approach, identify stakeholders.
KA-4: Analyze current state, define future state.
KA-5: Analyze, model, and validate requirements.
KA-3: Trace, prioritize, and approve requirements.
KA-5: Define design options, recommend solution.
KA-3: Update requirements based on learnings.

rule.statement: For a change request, jump directly to KA-3 (assess the change) and KA-5 (analyze impact on existing requirements). Skip KA-1 and KA-4 unless the change redefines the project scope or strategic goals.
rule.rationale: Repeating full KA sequencing for a scope change wastes time; the existing BA plan and strategy analysis remain valid unless the change is transformational.

section.title: Underlying Competencies — All KAs
section.p: Competencies required across all six knowledge areas:

list.item: Analytical — creative thinking, decision making, systems thinking, problem solving.
Business — business acumen, industry knowledge, organization knowledge.

rule.statement: Classify every BA request into one or more KAs before generating any deliverable. Use the KA routing result as shared state that all downstream agents read — never re-classify per task.
rule.rationale: Skipping the routing step causes agents to draft KA-5 specs when the actual gap is KA-1 stakeholder mapping; re-classifying per task produces inconsistent results across the pipeline.

antipattern.description: LLMs default to KA-5 (modelling) on any input.
antipattern.reason: Most real project failures are in KA-1 (no stakeholder map) or KA-3 (no traceability). Make the planner explicitly score all 6 KAs before dispatching.
